worth_it_explanation: Reporting accuracy decides whether the revenue and fill numbers a publisher acts on match the money that actually arrives. A report pulled in the wrong time zone, or read against the wrong dimensions, can show a revenue figure that never reconciles with the invoice or seller statement, so decisions about floors, demand partners, and inventory get made on numbers that do not tie out. The check is a review of report settings and a reconciliation the publisher can run inside Microsoft Monetize against documents they already receive, so it costs reading and cross-checking time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete settings or process fix.
intro: Reporting accuracy on Microsoft Monetize is about whether the revenue and fill numbers a publisher reads in the platform reconcile against the money Microsoft Advertising actually pays out, and whether reports are configured so the numbers compare cleanly across systems. Microsoft Monetize stores all backend data in Coordinated Universal Time (UTC) and bills in UTC, while reports can be run in a member or advertiser time zone, so the same activity can total differently depending on the settings chosen. These checks confirm that reported revenue and fill tie out to the Selling Billing Report, the invoice, and the seller statement, and that report dimensions and time zones are set so the figures stay comparable rather than drifting apart from one system to the next.

audit_checks.title: Reported revenue and fill are not reconciled against billing documents
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When reported revenue and fill are never tied back to the Selling Billing Report, the invoice, and the seller statement, a publisher cannot tell whether the numbers driving floor and demand decisions match the money that actually arrives. A persistent gap that goes unchecked can hide underpayment, misattributed fees, or a reporting setting that inflates a figure, so decisions get made on numbers that do not reconcile with the payout.

educate.base:
Microsoft Monetize separates the numbers a publisher reads day to day from the numbers that determine payment. The Selling Billing Report shows billable impressions, gross seller revenue, seller media cost, and the fees deducted from each, and it is the report Microsoft Advertising points publishers to when reconciling the invoice and the seller statement. The invoice lists the amount owed or paid for a billing period, and the seller statement nets buying and selling activity into the final figure. A publisher who reads revenue from a general analytics view but never ties it back to these billing documents has no confirmation that the reported number matches the money that arrives.

Reconciliation matters because the figures can diverge for reasons that are easy to miss. Billing data is stored in UTC, so a revenue total pulled in a local time zone covers a slightly different window than the billing period and will not match unless the report is aligned to UTC. Fees such as the Seller Auction Service Fee and the Ad Serving Fee for kept, blank, default, and Public Service Announcement (PSA) impressions reduce the seller media cost, and the Ad Serving Fee does not appear in the Selling Billing Report by default unless 'Filtered Requests' is selected under Metrics. Invalid traffic claims and credit memos adjust the final amount after the fact. Any one of these can open a gap between the revenue a publisher reads and the revenue Microsoft Advertising pays.

Microsoft Advertising documents the reconciliation directly. Its guidance walks through running the Selling Billing Report, aligning the time period to UTC, and matching the report's billable impressions and amount columns against the corresponding fields on the invoice. Microsoft Advertising states that the report values should match the invoice values for each charge, which gives a publisher a concrete tie-out to perform rather than an assumption that the numbers agree.

A few signs point to revenue and fill not being reconciled:
  • Revenue is read from a general or analytics report that is never matched against the Selling Billing Report, the invoice, or the seller statement
  • A recurring gap exists between reported revenue and the paid amount with no recorded explanation
  • The publisher cannot say whether the reported figures are aligned to UTC to match the billing period, or whether fees and invalid traffic adjustments were accounted for in the comparison
You may want to confirm that reported revenue and fill tie out to the billing documents for a recent period. One approach is to run the Selling Billing Report aligned to UTC for a closed billing month, then match its billable impressions and amount columns against the invoice and seller statement, recording any gap and its cause.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, revenue can swing with seasonal demand and direct-sold campaigns running alongside programmatic, which makes a clean monthly reconciliation more useful for spotting a real gap against background variation. You may want to reconcile the Selling Billing Report against the invoice each billing period and keep a record of any recurring difference and its cause, so a payout gap surfaces as a tracked item rather than a surprise.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, programmatic revenue often sits beside affiliate and sponsored placements that are billed outside Microsoft Monetize, so it helps to reconcile only the Monetize-billed activity rather than a blended revenue figure. You may want to match the Selling Billing Report's seller media cost against the seller statement for the Monetize portion specifically, so the reconciliation is not muddied by revenue that the platform never billed.

investigate.intro: Review whether reported revenue and fill are tied back to the Selling Billing Report, the invoice, and the seller statement for a recent billing period, and whether any gap is explained.

monetize_ui.label: Microsoft Monetize UI
steps: Go to 'Reporting', then 'Report Center', and run the Selling Billing Report for a recent closed billing month with the time period aligned to UTC.
Under 'Metrics', select 'Filtered Requests' so Ad Serving Fees for kept, blank, default, and PSA impressions appear, then note billable impressions, gross seller revenue, and seller media cost.
Open the corresponding invoice and seller statement for the same period and match the report's billable units and amount columns against the invoice fields.

Record any difference between the reported revenue and the billed or paid amount, along with its cause, such as a time zone misalignment, a fee not accounted for, or an invalid traffic claim adjustment.

monetize_reporting.label: Microsoft Monetize reporting
steps: Build a Selling Billing Report broken out by publisher and impression type so kept, PSA, default, and RTB impressions are visible separately.
Compare the fill picture across impression types against the billable impressions since unsold, kept, and PSA impressions affect fill but clear differently for revenue.
Note whether the revenue and fill figures the publisher reports elsewhere reconcile with this billing view or diverge from it.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for a recent closed billing period, whether reported revenue and fill were reconciled against the Selling Billing Report, the invoice, and the seller statement, and whether any gap has a recorded cause. A setup where the figures tie out within a small tolerance, or where any gap is explained by a documented fee, time zone alignment, or invalid traffic adjustment, passes. A setup where revenue is read without any tie-out to the billing documents, or where a recurring gap exists with no recorded cause, is a fail recorded with the period and the size of the gap.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether reported revenue and fill were reconciled against the Selling Billing Report, invoice, and seller statement for a recent period, and any gap and its cause above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} reads reported revenue and fill without reconciling them against the Selling Billing Report, the invoice, and the seller statement, so the numbers driving floor and demand decisions are not confirmed against the money Microsoft Advertising actually pays. Billing data is stored in Coordinated Universal Time (UTC), and fees such as the Seller Auction Service Fee and Ad Serving Fees reduce the payout, so a revenue figure read without a tie-out can diverge from the billed amount without anyone seeing it. {{voice_recommend}} running the Selling Billing Report aligned to UTC for a closed billing period, matching its billable impressions and amount columns against the invoice and seller statement, and recording any gap with its cause. After the first reconciliation, keep it as a recurring billing-period check so a payout gap surfaces as a tracked item.

audit_checks.title: Report time zone and dimensions are not aligned for cross-system comparison
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When reports are pulled in a local time zone while billing and partner systems report in UTC, daily totals spill across day boundaries and stop matching, so a publisher comparing Microsoft Monetize against an ad server or demand partner sees a gap that is an artifact of settings rather than a real discrepancy. Mismatched dimensions compound this by comparing figures that are not measuring the same thing, which sends time into chasing differences that the report configuration created.

educate.base:
Microsoft Monetize stores all backend reporting data in Coordinated Universal Time (UTC), and billing data is always in UTC, but a report can be run in the member or advertiser time zone. That flexibility is useful for reading numbers in local business hours, and it is also where cross-system comparisons go wrong. Most Monetize objects default to Eastern U.S. time, so a publisher who pulls a daily revenue report in Eastern time is grouping activity by a day boundary that does not match the UTC boundary the billing system and many demand partners use. The same impressions land in a different day, and the daily totals stop lining up.

Time zone support in Monetize reporting is available only for report types that include hourly data, and Daylight Saving Time adds two days a year where a local-time report shows a 23-hour or a 25-hour day because an hour is skipped or repeated. Microsoft Advertising recommends running reports in UTC when precise hourly visibility or a clean cross-system comparison is needed since UTC gives a full 24-hour view without the missing or doubled hour that local time introduces during the transitions.

Dimensions matter as much as time. A revenue figure grouped by one set of dimensions does not necessarily compare to a figure grouped another way, and metrics that look similar can measure different things. The Prebid Server Premium Health Analytics Report, for example, is built on sample data multiplied to estimate volume and is explicitly not intended for delivery or revenue reporting, so its impression counts will not match a billing report. Comparing across systems only works when the time zone, the date grouping, and the dimensions describe the same slice of activity in both.

A few signs point to reports not being aligned for comparison:
  • Daily reports are pulled in a local time zone, such as the Eastern default, then compared against an ad server or demand partner reporting in UTC
  • A recurring daily gap appears between Monetize and another system that shrinks or disappears when totals are summed over a longer period, which points to a day-boundary shift
  • Figures from reports grouped by different dimensions, or drawn from sample-based analytics reports, are compared against billing figures as though they measure the same thing
You may want to confirm that comparisons across systems use a common time basis and matching dimensions. One approach is to run the Monetize report in UTC, match its date grouping and dimensions to the other system, and compare over a window long enough to absorb any single-day boundary effect.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, traffic often peaks in evening local hours, which sit on either side of the UTC day boundary depending on the region, so a local-time daily report can shift a meaningful share of impressions into the adjacent day. You may want to run cross-system comparisons in UTC and compare over a week rather than a single day, so an apparent gap that is really a day-boundary shift does not get treated as a real discrepancy.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, shopping activity can cluster around specific local hours and around sale events that start at midnight local time, which lands right on a time zone boundary. You may want to align the report time zone to UTC when comparing Monetize against an ad server or demand partner, so a sale-day spike is counted in the same day across systems rather than split between two.

investigate.intro: Review the time zone and dimensions used when Microsoft Monetize reports are compared against other systems, and look for daily gaps that point to a day-boundary or dimension mismatch.

monetize_ui.label: Microsoft Monetize UI
steps: Go to 'Reporting', then 'Report Center', open the report used for cross-system comparison, and check the 'Timezone' setting under the advanced options.
Note whether the report is run in a local time zone, such as the Eastern default, or aligned to UTC, and whether the report type even supports a time zone selection, which requires hourly data.
Record the dimensions and the date grouping the report uses, and compare them against the dimensions and grouping of the system it is being matched against.
Confirm whether the comparison source is a billing report or a sample-based analytics report since the Prebid Server Premium Health Analytics Report is not intended for delivery or revenue reporting.

monetize_reporting.label: Microsoft Monetize reporting
steps: Run the comparison report once in the local time zone and once in UTC for the same period, and note how much the daily totals shift between the two.
Sum the totals over a week or a month to see whether a daily gap shrinks or disappears, which points to a day-boundary effect rather than a real discrepancy.
Note any case where reports grouped by different dimensions, or a sample-based report, are being compared against a billing figure.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the time zone, date grouping, and dimensions used for each cross-system comparison, and whether they match the system being compared against. A setup where comparisons are run in a common time basis, usually UTC to match billing and partner systems, with matching dimensions and a billing source for revenue figures, passes. A setup where a local-time report is compared against a UTC system, or where mismatched dimensions or a sample-based report are compared against billing figures, is a fail recorded with the report and the mismatch.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the time zone, date grouping, and dimensions used for cross-system comparisons and whether they match the other system above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} compares Microsoft Monetize reports against other systems without aligning the time zone and dimensions, so daily totals shift across the day boundary and stop matching. Monetize stores data and bills in Coordinated Universal Time (UTC) while reports can run in a local time zone, such as the Eastern default, so a daily comparison against a UTC system shows a gap that is an artifact of settings rather than a real discrepancy. {{voice_recommend}} running cross-system comparisons in UTC, matching the date grouping and dimensions to the other system, and comparing over a window long enough to absorb a single-day boundary effect. After aligning the settings, confirm that the remaining gap is small and stable before treating any difference as a real reporting problem.
